content.text: Accessing Resources

A managed identity provides an Azure resource with an OAuth 2.0 access token that it can use to authenticate to any Azure service that supports Entra ID token-based access. The resource never handles a password or client secret — the Azure platform handles token issuance transparently through the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint.

attrs.alt: Managed identity token acquisition flow: App Code → IMDS → Entra ID → JWT → Azure services

content.text: How Token Acquisition Works

When your application code calls an Azure SDK client, DefaultAzureCredential transparently requests a token by calling the local IMDS endpoint at 169.254.169.254. IMDS validates the request against the identity provisioned on the host and contacts Entra ID on the resource's behalf to exchange a signed certificate for a short-lived JWT access token. The SDK caches this token and refreshes it automatically before expiry.
App code instantiates DefaultAzureCredential or ManagedIdentityCredential.
SDK calls GET http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?resource=<target>.
IMDS validates the caller is running on the correct Azure resource.
IMDS contacts Entra ID with the resource's identity certificate.
Entra ID issues a signed JWT access token scoped to the target resource.
SDK caches the token and attaches it as an Authorization: Bearer header on every API call.
The target service validates the token signature and checks the caller's RBAC role.

The IMDS endpoint is only reachable from inside Azure-hosted compute. For local development, DefaultAzureCredential falls through to Azure CLI credentials or environment-variable credentials automatically.
RBAC Role Assignment — Pre-condition

A token alone is not enough to access a resource. The managed identity's principal ID must hold an appropriate RBAC role on the target scope before the token is issued. The target service validates both the token signature and the role assignment at the time of each API call.
Target Resource
Required Role (minimum)
Notes
Azure Key Vault (secrets)
Key Vault Secrets User
Read secret values; does not allow listing or managing
Azure Key Vault (keys for crypto)
Key Vault Crypto User
Sign / verify / encrypt / decrypt
Azure Storage (blobs)
Storage Blob Data Reader / Contributor
Reader for read-only; Contributor allows write
Azure Storage (queues)
Storage Queue Data Reader / Contributor
Azure SQL
db_datareader / db_datawriter
Granted inside the DB with CREATE USER FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER
Azure Service Bus
Azure Service Bus Data Receiver / Sender
Separate roles for send vs receive
Azure Event Hubs
Azure Event Hubs Data Receiver / Sender
Azure Cosmos DB
Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader / Contributor
Assigned via Cosmos DB RBAC, not ARM RBAC
Accessing Azure Key Vault

Key Vault is the most common target for managed identity access. The SDK handles token acquisition and renewal automatically — your code only interacts with the SecretClient, KeyClient, or CertificateClient.
Python

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = SecretClient(
    vault_url='https://my-vault.vault.azure.net',
    credential=credential
)

# Retrieve a secret — token acquired transparently
secret = client.get_secret('db-connection-string')
print(secret.value)

# List secret names (requires Key Vault Secrets Officer or higher)
for s in client.list_properties_of_secrets():
    print(s.name, s.enabled)
C#
Node.js
Accessing Azure Blob Storage

To read or write blobs, assign the managed identity the Storage Blob Data Reader or Storage Blob Data Contributor role on the storage account (or a specific container). The SDK uses the same DefaultAzureCredential and the token is scoped to storage.azure.com.
Python — Upload and Download

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
service = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url='https://mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows.net',
    credential=credential
)

# Upload
container = service.get_container_client('my-container')
with open('/tmp/report.pdf', 'rb') as f:
    container.upload_blob('reports/2024-q4.pdf', f, overwrite=True)

# Download
blob = container.get_blob_client('reports/2024-q4.pdf')
data = blob.download_blob().readall()
C# — Stream Blob
Accessing Azure SQL

Azure SQL supports Entra ID token-based authentication. You do not supply a username/password in the connection string — instead, the driver acquires a token and sends it during the TDS handshake. You must first create an Entra ID user inside the database mapped to the managed identity.
Step 1 — Create the Database User
Step 2 — Connect from Python

import struct
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
import pyodbc

SQL_COPT_SS_ACCESS_TOKEN = 1256
AZURE_SQL_RESOURCE = 'https://database.windows.net/'

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token(AZURE_SQL_RESOURCE).token

# Encode token as expected by ODBC driver
token_bytes = token.encode('utf-16-le')
token_struct = struct.pack(f'<I{len(token_bytes)}s', len(token_bytes), token_bytes)

conn_str = (
    'Driver={ODBC Driver 18 for SQL Server};'
    'Server=myserver.database.windows.net;'
    'Database=mydb;'
    'Encrypt=yes;'
    'TrustServerCertificate=no;'
)
conn = pyodbc.connect(conn_str, attrs_before={SQL_COPT_SS_ACCESS_TOKEN: token_struct})
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute('SELECT TOP 5 * FROM Orders')
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    print(row)
Step 2 (alternative) — C# with SqlClient
Accessing Azure Service Bus

Service Bus supports Entra ID token-based access through the AMQP protocol. Assign the identity either Azure Service Bus Data Sender (send messages) or Azure Service Bus Data Receiver (receive messages), or Azure Service Bus Data Owner for both plus management operations.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.servicebus import ServiceBusClient, ServiceBusMessage

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
fully_qualified_namespace = 'my-namespace.servicebus.windows.net'

with ServiceBusClient(fully_qualified_namespace, credential) as client:
    # Send
    with client.get_queue_sender('my-queue') as sender:
        sender.send_messages(ServiceBusMessage('Hello from managed identity'))

    # Receive
    with client.get_queue_receiver('my-queue', max_wait_time=5) as receiver:
        for msg in receiver:
            print(str(msg))
            receiver.complete_message(msg)
Raw IMDS Token Request
In environments without an Azure SDK (shell scripts, legacy runtimes), you can call the IMDS REST endpoint directly and parse the JSON response to extract the access_token field.

# Request a token scoped to Azure Key Vault
RESPONSE=$(curl -s -H 'Metadata: true' \
  'http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2018-02-01&resource=https://vault.azure.net')

TOKEN=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | python3 -c 'import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)["access_token"])')
EXPIRES=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | python3 -c 'import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)["expires_on"])')
echo "Token expires: $EXPIRES"

# Call Key Vault with the raw token
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  'https://my-vault.vault.azure.net/secrets/db-connection-string?api-version=7.4' | python3 -m json.tool

# Request a token for a user-assigned identity (specify client ID)
UA_CLIENT_ID='aaaabbbb-cccc-dddd-eeee-ffffffffffff'

curl -s -H 'Metadata: true' \
  "http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token" \
  --data-urlencode "api-version=2018-02-01" \
  --data-urlencode "resource=https://storage.azure.com/" \
  --data-urlencode "client_id=$UA_CLIENT_ID" | python3 -m json.tool
Local Development with DefaultAzureCredential

DefaultAzureCredential tries multiple credential sources in order. In production it reaches IMDS; locally it falls through to Azure CLI credentials, VS Code login, or environment variables. This means the same code works in both environments without any branching.
Environment
Credential Source Used
How to Configure
Azure VM / App Service / Function
IMDS (managed identity)
Enable identity on the resource
Local dev (Azure CLI logged in)
Azure CLI credentials
az login
Local dev (VS Code)
VS Code Azure extension
Sign in via VS Code Azure panel
CI/CD (GitHub Actions, Azure DevOps)
Environment variable credential
Set AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET, AZURE_TENANT_ID
Docker container (not on Azure)
Environment variable credential or Workload Identity
Mount AZURE_* env vars or use AKS workload identity

DefaultAzureCredential probes each source in fixed order and skips sources that are not configured. If IMDS is unavailable (not on Azure), it moves on to CLI credentials automatically — no code change required.
Token Caching and Refresh

The Azure SDKs cache access tokens for their lifetime (typically 60–75 minutes) and refresh them automatically before expiry. You do not need to implement token caching or renewal in your application code. Token cache is held in memory per-process and is not persisted to disk by default.
Token lifetime is set by Entra ID and is typically 60–75 minutes for managed identity tokens.
The SDK refreshes the token 5 minutes before the cached token expires.
If IMDS is temporarily unavailable during refresh, the SDK retries with exponential backoff.
Each SDK client instance manages its own token cache — if you instantiate multiple clients, each will have its own cache. Share the credential object to share the cache.
For high-throughput scenarios, create a single credential and client instance per application lifetime, not per request.
Accessing Azure Cosmos DB

Cosmos DB uses its own built-in RBAC system (not ARM RBAC) to authorize managed identities. You assign a Cosmos DB built-in data role using the Azure CLI or REST API, then use DefaultAzureCredential with the CosmosClient. Connection strings with account keys are not required.
Assign Cosmos DB Role

# Get the managed identity's principal ID
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az webapp identity show \
    --resource-group MyRG \
    --name MyApp \
    --query principalId --output tsv)

ACCOUNT_ID=$(az cosmosdb show \
    --resource-group MyRG \
    --name mycosmosaccount \
    --query id --output tsv)

# Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor (read + write)
az cosmosdb sql role assignment create \
    --account-name mycosmosaccount \
    --resource-group MyRG \
    --role-definition-id '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002' \
    --principal-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --scope "$ACCOUNT_ID"

# Built-in role IDs:
# 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001 = Cosmos DB Built-in Data Reader
# 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002 = Cosmos DB Built-in Data Contributor
Python — Query Cosmos DB

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.cosmos import CosmosClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = CosmosClient(
    url='https://mycosmosaccount.documents.azure.com:443/',
    credential=credential
)

db = client.get_database_client('mydb')
container = db.get_container_client('orders')

query = 'SELECT * FROM c WHERE c.status = @status'
items = list(container.query_items(
    query=query,
    parameters=[{'name': '@status', 'value': 'pending'}],
    enable_cross_partition_query=True
))
for item in items:
    print(item['id'], item['status'])
Accessing Azure Event Hubs

Event Hubs integrates with Entra ID through the Azure.Messaging.EventHubs SDK. Assign the identity Azure Event Hubs Data Sender to publish events, Azure Event Hubs Data Receiver to consume events, or Azure Event Hubs Data Owner for full management access.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.eventhub import EventHubProducerClient, EventData
from azure.eventhub import EventHubConsumerClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
namespace = 'my-namespace.servicebus.windows.net'
eventhub_name = 'telemetry'

# Produce events
with EventHubProducerClient(namespace, eventhub_name, credential) as producer:
    batch = producer.create_batch()
    batch.add(EventData('{"sensor": "temp", "value": 22.4}'))
    producer.send_batch(batch)

# Consume events
def on_event(partition_context, event):
    print(f'Partition {partition_context.partition_id}: {event.body_as_str()}')
    partition_context.update_checkpoint(event)

with EventHubConsumerClient(
    namespace, eventhub_name, '$Default', credential
) as consumer:
    consumer.receive(on_event=on_event, starting_position='-1')
Terraform — Assign RBAC via IaC

When provisioning managed identities and role assignments via Terraform, define both the identity and the role assignment in the same module. Terraform captures the principal_id from the resource creation output and passes it directly to azurerm_role_assignment.
KQL: Audit Token Issuance and Resource Access

Entra ID and Azure resource diagnostic logs record managed identity token issuance and resource access events. These queries help you verify which identities are accessing which services and catch anomalous access patterns.
Managed Identity Sign-In Events

// Managed identity token acquisitions over the last 7 days
// Shows which resource (service principal) acquired tokens and for which target
AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where ServicePrincipalType == 'ManagedIdentity'
| summarize
    Calls         = count(),
    Targets       = make_set(ResourceDisplayName),
    FirstSeen     = min(TimeGenerated),
    LastSeen      = max(TimeGenerated)
    by ServicePrincipalName, ServicePrincipalId
| order by Calls desc
Key Vault Secret Access Audit

// Key Vault secret access events — shows who read what secret
// Requires Key Vault diagnostic logs sent to Log Analytics
AzureDiagnostics
| where ResourceType == 'VAULTS'
| where OperationName == 'SecretGet'
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| extend SecretName = tostring(id_s)
| extend CallerPrincipal = tostring(identity_claim_upn_s)
|          // For managed identities, CallerPrincipal is the object ID
| summarize
    Reads = count(),
    LastRead = max(TimeGenerated)
    by SecretName, CallerPrincipal, ResultType
| where ResultType == 'Success'
| order by Reads desc
Storage Blob Access Audit

// Storage blob read/write operations by managed identities
// Requires storage account diagnostic logs (StorageBlobLogs)
StorageBlobLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)
| where AuthenticationType == 'OAuth'
| where OperationName in ('GetBlob', 'PutBlob', 'DeleteBlob')
| summarize
    Ops = count(),
    Blobs = make_set(ObjectKey)
    by OperationName, CallerIpAddress, AccountName, bin(TimeGenerated, 1h)
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Common Errors and Fixes
Error
Cause
Fix
AuthorizationFailed: does not have ... authorization to perform action
RBAC role not assigned or propagation delay
Assign correct role; wait up to 5 minutes for propagation
CredentialUnavailableError: ManagedIdentityCredential authentication unavailable
IMDS not reachable — running outside Azure without fallback configured
On Azure: enable managed identity on the resource. Local: az login or set AZURE_* env vars
ManagedIdentityCredential: AKS workload identity not configured
Running in AKS without workload identity federation
Configure AKS workload identity; set AZURE_CLIENT_ID + projected service account token
KeyVaultError: Forbidden (access policy)
Key Vault uses access policies (legacy) instead of RBAC, and identity not in policy
Switch Key Vault to RBAC permission model, or add identity to access policy
SqlException: Login failed for user 'NT AUTHORITY\ANONYMOUS LOGON'
SQL connection string uses SQL auth or identity not mapped in the database
Remove username/password from conn string; run CREATE USER FROM EXTERNAL PROVIDER in the DB
Token expiry during long-running operation
Token cached at start, operation exceeds 75-minute lifetime
Obtain a fresh token per operation batch; do not cache credentials manually
Summary

A managed identity accesses Azure resources through three components working together: the IMDS endpoint (which the Azure SDK calls automatically), Entra ID (which issues a short-lived JWT), and RBAC role assignments (which authorize the identity on the target resource). The same DefaultAzureCredential code path works in production (using IMDS) and local development (using CLI credentials) with no application changes. Key Vault, Storage, SQL, Service Bus, and most other Azure services accept the same bearer token pattern — the main setup difference is which RBAC role to assign on each target.
